---
title: "SVD and Low-Rank Approximation"
---

## Role

The singular value decomposition is one of the most useful structural factorizations in applied math.

## Intuition

SVD says a matrix can be understood as:

- a rotation or change of coordinates
- a stretching along orthogonal directions
- another rotation

That turns an opaque matrix into geometry you can reason about.

## Formal Core

For a matrix `A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}`, the SVD is

$$
A = U \Sigma V^\top,
$$

where `U` and `V` have orthonormal columns and `\Sigma` contains the singular values.

::: {#thm-eckart-young}
## Eckart-Young Intuition

Keeping only the top singular directions gives the best low-rank approximation to `A` in spectral and Frobenius senses.
:::

## Why It Matters

This is the mathematical core behind:

- PCA
- latent factor models
- compression
- denoising
- low-rank methods in optimization and ML

## Computation Lens

SVD is not only a theorem. It is also a computational tool, and in large problems you immediately start caring about approximation quality, randomized algorithms, and numerical stability.

## Paper Bridge

Low-rank adaptation, matrix sketching, spectral clustering, and many representation-learning papers all rely on SVD thinking even when the paper language is more specialized.
